heroOverview: Ophiasis is a severe and treatment-resistant subtype of alopecia areata in which hair loss forms a continuous band along the lower scalp margins — the occipital, temporal, and parietal hairline — tracing the outline of a snake, as the Greek name implies. It is estimated to affect 3-5% of all alopecia areata patients and carries a substantially worse prognosis than patchy alopecia areata, with spontaneous remission rates below 10% compared with up to 50-80% in the patchy form. Like all alopecia areata subtypes, ophiasis is an autoimmune disorder in which T-lymphocytes attack hair follicles in the anagen (growth) phase. The band distribution reflects preferential immune targeting of follicles in the scalp margins and correlates with reduced treatment response to conventional therapies, though newer JAK inhibitor treatments are showing promising results for previously refractory cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Ophiasis accounts for approximately 3-5% of alopecia areata cases; alopecia areata itself affects roughly 2% of the global population at some point in their lifetime
keyStats.demographics: Affects both sexes equally; can occur at any age, but onset in childhood or adolescence is associated with greater severity and higher rates of the ophiasis pattern
keyStats.avgAge: No specific peak age for ophiasis; onset before puberty is associated with higher risk of progressing to this form
keyStats.globalCases: Alopecia areata affects an estimated 147 million people worldwide; ophiasis represents the most common severe margin-pattern variant

definition: Ophiasis (ICD-10: L63.2) is a patterned subtype of alopecia areata characterised by a continuous band of non-scarring hair loss running along the margins of the scalp — typically beginning occipitally and extending bilaterally across the temporal and parietal zones to produce a serpentine fringe of alopecia immediately above the ears and around the nape. The word derives from the Greek ophis (snake), reflecting the sinuous band shape. Like patchy alopecia areata (ICD-10: L63.9), ophiasis is a T-lymphocyte-mediated autoimmune attack on anagen-phase hair follicles, driven by collapse of immune privilege in the follicle bulb. The immune cells recognise follicular antigens, particularly within the outer root sheath, and precipitate premature termination of the anagen cycle. Unlike the diffuse or patchy forms, ophiasis-pattern follicles in the lower scalp margins appear particularly resistant to standard immunosuppressive therapies, a characteristic attributed to anatomical differences in the follicular immune microenvironment in this region. Ophiasis falls under dermatology and, specifically, trichology. It is non-scarring, meaning follicles are preserved and regrowth remains theoretically possible even in long-standing cases — a feature that distinguishes it from the cicatricial alopecias such as pseudopelade and lichen planopilaris.

typesClassification.description: Continuous band of alopecia along the occipital and temporal margins, leaving a fringe of scalp hair above the ears and at the nape. The most common pattern.
Hair loss in the central scalp while the marginal fringe is spared, the reverse of the classic pattern. Sisaipho is ophiasis spelled backwards. Equally treatment-resistant.
The band extends inward as the disease progresses toward alopecia totalis. This transitional form may fluctuate between ophiasis and near-total involvement.
Patients with atopy (asthma, eczema, hay fever) and ophiasis tend to have an earlier onset and more persistent disease course than non-atopic individuals.

primarySymptoms: A continuous band of smooth, non-scarring hair loss running along the lower scalp margin above the ears, at the temples, and across the occipital scalp.
Loss of the hairline fringe at the nape of the neck, often the earliest and most noticeable feature reported by patients.
Exclamation-mark hairs — short, broken hairs that taper at the base — visible at the active advancing border of the alopecia band.
Smooth, normal-looking skin within the bald area without scaling, redness, or follicular plugging, distinguishing it from scarring alopecia.
Positive pull test at the border of the hair loss area, where gentle traction dislodges telogen hairs more easily than normal.
Nail pitting or trachyonychia (rough, sandpaper-like nails) in a subset of patients, reflecting the systemic autoimmune process.
Preserved hair in the central and vertex scalp in early cases, contrasting with the marginal band of loss.
Occasional mild scalp tingling or itching reported by approximately 10-20% of patients at the onset of active disease.
Loss of eyebrow and eyelash hair in cases progressing toward alopecia totalis or universalis, particularly in children.
Regrowth of fine, unpigmented vellus hairs within the alopecic band in partially responding cases, sometimes followed by repigmentation.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing difficulty styling hair around the temples or occipital region where the hairline appears to be receding symmetrically.
Finding more short broken hairs than usual on pillows or in the shower drain from the nape area.
A subtle widening of the part line or thinning at the lower scalp margins that progresses over several weeks.
Fine, white or colourless regrowth hairs within a previously bare patch, indicating disease activity.
emergencySigns: Rapid extension of the alopecia band within days to weeks, spreading to involve the entire scalp (alopecia totalis), which requires urgent dermatology referral.
Loss of all eyebrows and eyelashes along with total scalp hair loss, suggesting progression to alopecia universalis.
New psychological crisis, acute anxiety, or depressive episode precipitated by hair loss, which requires prompt mental health support alongside dermatology management.

causes.cause: Autoimmune T-lymphocyte attack on anagen hair follicles
causes.description: CD8+ T cells and natural killer cells infiltrate the anagen follicle bulb and destroy the immune privilege of the follicular epithelium. They recognise follicular antigens — likely including melanocyte-associated epitopes — and secrete interferon-gamma, triggering premature termination of the growth cycle. This mechanism is shared with all alopecia areata subtypes but is anatomically concentrated in scalp margin follicles in ophiasis.

Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified more than 20 genomic loci associated with alopecia areata, including genes involved in T-cell activation, regulatory T-cell function, and HLA antigen presentation. First-degree relatives of affected individuals have a lifetime risk approximately five times higher than the general population.

Patients with atopic dermatitis, asthma, or hay fever have significantly higher rates of alopecia areata, and those who develop alopecia areata in the setting of atopy are more likely to develop the severe ophiasis or totalis pattern. The shared IL-4 and IL-13 pathways between atopy and alopecia areata provide a mechanistic link.

causes.cause: Psychological and physiological stress as a trigger
causes.description: Clinical reports and epidemiological studies associate onset or relapse of alopecia areata with acute major stress events, serious illness, bereavement, and post-surgical recovery. Stress activates corticotrophin-releasing hormone in the perifollicular skin, which can modulate the local immune environment and reduce follicular immune privilege.

Preclinical and clinical research has established that interferon-gamma activates the JAK1/JAK2 and STAT1/STAT3 pathways in follicular keratinocytes, leading to immune privilege collapse. This mechanistic understanding underpins the clinical effectiveness of JAK inhibitors in treating alopecia areata and particularly severe subtypes like ophiasis.

causes.cause: Thyroid autoimmunity and other systemic autoimmune comorbidities
causes.description: Thyroid autoimmune disorders (Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease), vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis co-occur with alopecia areata more frequently than expected by chance, suggesting shared genetic susceptibility loci and immune dysregulation pathways.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of alopecia areata
riskFactors.description: Having a first-degree relative with alopecia areata raises lifetime risk approximately five-fold. Specific HLA class II alleles (HLA-DQB1*03, HLA-DRB1*04) are associated with more severe and extensive forms including ophiasis.

riskFactors.factor: Atopic dermatitis or other atopic disease
riskFactors.description: Atopy is one of the strongest predictors of severe alopecia areata. Patients with atopic dermatitis who develop alopecia areata have a two- to three-fold higher likelihood of the extensive ophiasis or totalis pattern compared with non-atopic patients.
Onset of alopecia areata before age 16 is associated with a higher frequency of the ophiasis pattern and a lower rate of complete spontaneous remission than adult-onset disease.

Alopecia areata, often in severe forms, occurs in approximately 8-10% of individuals with Down syndrome — several times the population baseline — likely due to immune dysregulation associated with the trisomy.

Major life stressors, sleep deprivation, and acute illness have been identified as triggers for onset or relapse in case-controlled studies. Stress management may reduce relapse frequency though direct evidence is limited.

Thyroid autoimmune disorders, vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis each confer an increased relative risk of alopecia areata development and are associated with more refractory disease courses.

A history of patchy alopecia areata is the strongest individual predictor of a subsequent ophiasis episode; ophiasis rarely presents as the first-ever episode, typically following repeated patchy cycles.

affectedDemographics: Children and adolescents in whom early-onset alopecia areata preferentially takes the severe ophiasis or totalis form
Adults of any age with a history of relapsing-remitting patchy alopecia areata
Individuals with personal or family history of atopic dermatitis, asthma, or hay fever
People with Down syndrome, in whom severe alopecia areata is substantially more common
Patients with coexisting autoimmune thyroid disease, vitiligo, or type 1 diabetes
Both sexes equally — ophiasis does not show the pronounced sex difference seen in androgenic alopecia
diagnosisOverview:
Ophiasis is a clinical diagnosis in the majority of cases. The characteristic continuous band of smooth, non-scarring alopecia along the scalp margins is diagnostically distinctive when seen by a dermatologist or trichologist. The clinician notes the distribution of hair loss, the appearance of the scalp skin within the bald area (smooth and normal in ophiasis versus scarred or atrophic in cicatricial alopecias), exclamation-mark hairs at the advancing border, and any nail changes. Dermoscopy is used to confirm the alopecia areata pattern: yellow dots (empty follicles), black dots (cadaverised hairs), short vellus hairs, and exclamation-mark hairs are the key features. A pull test at the active margin quantifies ongoing disease activity.

When the diagnosis is uncertain, a 4 mm punch biopsy from the active border shows a characteristic 'swarm of bees' perifollicular lymphocytic infiltrate on histology, with preserved follicle number distinguishing ophiasis from scarring alopecias. Blood tests are not required for diagnosis but are performed to identify autoimmune comorbidities: thyroid function (TSH, free T4), anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies, antinuclear antibody (ANA), full blood count, and ferritin (to exclude iron deficiency as a concurrent contributor). The principal differential diagnoses are tinea capitis (scalp ringworm), trichotillomania, traction alopecia affecting the hairline, and marginal traction alopecia from tight hairstyling.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms alopecia areata pattern by identifying yellow dots, black dots, exclamation-mark hairs, and short vellus hairs at the active border without dermoscopic signs of fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist applies a handheld or video dermatoscope to the scalp; the painless examination takes 5-10 minutes and does not require any preparation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies active hair shedding at the margins of the alopecia band; a positive test (more than 6 hairs per pull) indicates ongoing disease activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician grasps approximately 60 hairs near the bald margin and pulls with gentle continuous traction; the number of hairs removed in one pull is counted.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates ophiasis from scarring alopecias (lichen planopilaris, pseudopelade) and from other causes of marginal hair loss when the diagnosis is uncertain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small circular skin sample is taken under local anaesthetic from the active border; it is processed for haematoxylin-eosin and periodic acid-Schiff staining; results take 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for Hashimoto's thyroiditis or Graves' disease, which co-occur with alopecia areata at higher than expected rates and can themselves contribute to diffuse hair shedding.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes systemic lupus erythematosus, which can cause a discoid scalp involvement that superficially resembles ophiasis in distribution.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw tested by immunofluorescence; positive results at titre ≥1:160 warrant further specific antibody testing.

diagnosticTests.test: Wood's lamp examination and fungal culture of scalp scraping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes tinea capitis (scalp ringworm) as a cause of marginal scalp alopecia, particularly in children. Tinea capitis causes inflammatory or non-inflammatory patchy hair loss with scalp scaling and fluoresces under Wood's lamp with some species.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician shines a UV light on the scalp in a darkened room; scalp scraping is sent for KOH preparation and culture. Painless and takes a few minutes.

treatmentOverview:
Ophiasis is one of the most treatment-resistant forms of alopecia areata, and the response to conventional immunosuppressive therapies used for patchy alopecia areata — including intralesional corticosteroid injections and topical minoxidil — is significantly lower. Realistic treatment goals include slowing progression and inducing partial regrowth rather than guaranteeing complete cure.

For mild to moderate ophiasis, topical high-potency corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate 0.05% solution or foam) applied daily to the alopecic band are the starting point. Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide injections into the scalp margin every 4-6 weeks are a standard second-line approach but require significant surface area to cover in the band distribution. Topical minoxidil 5% solution or foam, applied twice daily, may support follicle cycling and is used as an adjunct. Contact immunotherapy with diphenylcyclopropenone (DPCP) or squaric acid dibutyl ester (SADBE) is an established dermatologist-administered treatment in which the scalp is sensitised to a topical allergen; local allergic dermatitis is used to redirect the immune response away from the follicle. Response rates of 30-60% have been reported for severe alopecia areata in specialist centres.

The most significant advance in treatment is the JAK inhibitor class. Baricitinib (oral, 2 mg or 4 mg daily) received FDA approval in 2022 for severe alopecia areata, and ritlecitinib (an oral JAK3/TEC inhibitor) received FDA approval in 2023 specifically for severe alopecia areata including ophiasis in patients aged 12 and over. Clinical trials of both agents showed meaningful scalp hair coverage in 30-40% of treated patients at week 36. These agents are systemic immunomodulators requiring screening for tuberculosis, lipids, and blood count monitoring during treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical high-potency corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% solution, foam, or ointment applied once or twice daily to the bald scalp margins. First-line treatment for mild ophiasis. Reduces perifollicular inflammation and can stabilise the disease margin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant regrowth in approximately 20-30% of ophiasis patients with consistent use; lower response than for patchy alopecia areata.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide injections
medicalTreatments.description: Triamcinolone 5-10 mg/mL injected into multiple sites along the alopecia band every 4-6 weeks. The spatial extent of ophiasis makes this less practical than in patchy disease but it remains a standard second-line option.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Hair regrowth observed in approximately 30-40% of treated sites in specialist reports; requires persistent treatment for several months.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical minoxidil 5%
medicalTreatments.description: Applied twice daily to the bald scalp as an adjunct to immunosuppressive treatment. Minoxidil prolongs the anagen cycle of recovering follicles. It does not address the autoimmune mechanism but can amplify regrowth when combined with other therapies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: An adjunct that improves response rates when combined with intralesional corticosteroids or immunotherapy; modest evidence for ophiasis-specific benefit as monotherapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Contact immunotherapy (DPCP or SADBE)
medicalTreatments.description: The scalp is sensitised to diphenylcyclopropenone (DPCP) by the dermatologist, then weekly application at progressively higher concentrations maintains a controlled mild allergic contact dermatitis, redirecting the immune attack away from the hair follicle. Administered exclusively in specialist centres.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cosmetically acceptable regrowth in approximately 30-60% of patients with extensive alopecia areata including ophiasis in long-term cohort studies from specialist centres.

medicalTreatments.name: Baricitinib (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral JAK inhibitor (2 mg or 4 mg daily) that blocks interferon-gamma signalling and restores follicular immune privilege. FDA-approved in 2022 for adults with severe alopecia areata. Requires pre-treatment screening and regular monitoring for infection, lipids, and haematology.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the BRAVE-AA1 and BRAVE-AA2 trials, 35-38% of patients on 4 mg daily achieved SALT ≤20 (substantial scalp coverage) at 36 weeks, compared with 5% on placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Ritlecitinib (JAK3/TEC inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral selective JAK3 and TEC family kinase inhibitor (50 mg daily) FDA-approved in 2023 for severe alopecia areata, including ophiasis, in patients aged 12 and over. A narrower kinase profile than baricitinib may reduce some class-effect risks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the ALLEGRO trial, approximately 23% of patients achieved SALT ≤20 at 24 weeks on 50 mg daily; responses improved further by week 48 in long-term extension data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scalp camouflage products (fibres, powders, topical pigments)
alternativeTreatments.description: Keratin fibre sprays and scalp pigmentation products can reduce the visual impact of the bald band while medical treatment takes effect or while disease is monitored. They are not disease-modifying but can significantly reduce psychosocial impact.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wigs, hairpieces, and hair integration systems
alternativeTreatments.description: High-quality wigs and partial hairpieces provide a cosmetically effective solution and are medically recognised. In some countries they are available on prescription or with insurance coverage for severe alopecia areata.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological support and support groups
alternativeTreatments.description: Cognitive-behavioural therapy and peer support from alopecia-specific organisations (such as the National Alopecia Areata Foundation) reduce anxiety and depression associated with hair loss, which are reported in up to 40% of patients with extensive disease.

whySeeSpecialist: Ophiasis should be managed by a dermatologist with trichology expertise. The pattern's treatment resistance means empirical primary care treatment is unlikely to be sufficient; a specialist can confirm the diagnosis by dermoscopy or biopsy, exclude mimics including tinea capitis and traction alopecia, assess autoimmune comorbidities, and provide access to contact immunotherapy and JAK inhibitor therapy. Patients aged 12-17 with severe ophiasis should be referred specifically for ritlecitinib eligibility assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a dermatologist who has a dedicated hair loss clinic or trichology practice and who performs dermoscopy and scalp biopsy on site. Access to DPCP or SADBE contact immunotherapy is a marker of specialist-level care. For JAK inhibitor prescription, the practice should be set up for pre-treatment screening (QuantiFERON-TB, lipid panel, haematology) and regular monitoring. Patient advocacy organisations such as the National Alopecia Areata Foundation maintain international dermatologist directories.
preventionStrategies: There is no proven strategy to prevent ophiasis in a patient known to have alopecia areata.
Managing confirmed autoimmune thyroid disease optimally may reduce the overall inflammatory burden.
Avoiding tight hairstyling practices that stress the scalp margin (tight braids, weaves, high ponytails) reduces traction alopecia, which can overlap with and confuse the marginal pattern.
Attending regular follow-up with a trichologist allows early detection of disease activity, enabling prompt treatment before extensive band formation.
Addressing psychological stressors and prioritising sleep hygiene may reduce relapse frequency, though evidence for this specific effect in ophiasis is observational.
lifestyleModifications: Use a wide-tooth comb and avoid heat styling tools on the scalp margin where hair is regrowing.
Avoid harsh chemical treatments (perms, bleach, tight braids) on the hairline margins while disease is active.
Protect the scalp from sun exposure with a hat, particularly during JAK inhibitor therapy which can slightly increase photosensitivity.
Keep a hair loss diary noting the distribution and any apparent triggers; this information helps the dermatologist track activity and treatment response.
Join a patient support community — peer support reduces isolation and provides practical tips from others managing the same condition.

recommended: Adequate dietary iron (red meat, dark leafy greens, fortified cereals) — iron deficiency does not cause ophiasis but can exacerbate concurrent telogen effluvium and reduce hair density
Sufficient zinc and vitamin D, both of which modulate immune function and have been found to be lower in some alopecia areata patients compared with controls
A balanced anti-inflammatory dietary pattern with plentiful vegetables, legumes, whole grains, and omega-3 sources
avoid: No specific dietary restriction is proven to prevent or treat ophiasis
Crash dieting or severely calorie-restricted diets that may trigger or worsen concurrent telogen effluvium
Excess biotin supplementation without clinical indication, as megadoses can interfere with thyroid and hormone laboratory assays

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise has no contraindications in ophiasis and is actively encouraged, both for general wellbeing and for the stress-reducing benefits that may indirectly support a less reactive disease course. Wearing a hat or sports headband to cover the alopecia band can reduce self-consciousness and sun exposure during outdoor exercise. There are no restrictions on type or intensity of physical activity.
dailyManagement: Apply prescribed topical treatment to the band area after shampooing and drying the scalp, as directed by the dermatologist.
Use a gentle, sulphate-free shampoo to avoid scalp irritation that might be confused with treatment side effects.
Take photographs of the bald band monthly in identical lighting to compare coverage over time — response to JAK inhibitors can take 3-6 months to become visible.
Attend blood monitoring appointments on schedule if taking baricitinib or ritlecitinib.
Contact the treating dermatologist promptly if a new infection (respiratory, skin, or urinary) develops during JAK inhibitor therapy, as dosing adjustment may be needed.
prognosis: Ophiasis carries a substantially worse prognosis than patchy alopecia areata. Spontaneous complete remission occurs in fewer than 10% of patients with established ophiasis, compared with 50-80% for single-patch disease. Many patients experience a chronic, fluctuating course with periods of relative stability alternating with extension of the band. Up to 30% of ophiasis patients progress eventually to alopecia totalis or universalis, particularly those with childhood onset, strong atopic background, or Down syndrome. The advent of JAK inhibitors has changed this outlook for many patients: baricitinib and ritlecitinib produce clinically meaningful scalp coverage in 23-38% of treated patients and represent the first therapies to demonstrate consistent, sustained benefit in this subtype. Long-term safety data are still accumulating. Psychological impact is significant and often underestimated — depression and anxiety affect up to 40% of patients with severe alopecia areata and should be proactively assessed and addressed.
recoveryTimeline: Initial visible regrowth on JAK inhibitor therapy typically appears at weeks 12-16 and continues to improve through week 36 and beyond; patients are advised to continue treatment for at least 6 months before assessing response. Contact immunotherapy requires weekly clinic visits and 3-6 months before assessable regrowth. For patients who achieve regrowth, maintenance therapy is usually needed because disease returns in the majority on stopping treatment. Spontaneous partial regrowth, when it occurs, may appear within 6-12 months but relapse in the same band distribution is common.
complications: Progression to alopecia totalis (complete scalp hair loss) in approximately 20-30% of patients with untreated or refractory ophiasis.
Progression to alopecia universalis (total scalp, body, and eyelash/eyebrow hair loss) in a subset, carrying profound psychosocial impact.
Depression and anxiety, which occur in up to 40% of patients with extensive alopecia areata and require active psychological assessment and support.
Opportunistic infections during JAK inhibitor therapy, including reactivation of latent tuberculosis or herpes zoster; pre-treatment screening and monitoring mitigate this risk.
Permanent follicle damage in extremely long-standing disease, though true follicle destruction is far less common than in scarring alopecias and regrowth remains possible even after many years.

supportResources.name: National Alopecia Areata Foundation (NAAF)
supportResources.description: The leading US patient advocacy organisation for alopecia areata, providing information, physician directories, support groups, and research updates.

supportResources.name: Alopecia UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, peer support, and a helpline for people with all forms of alopecia areata including ophiasis.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Alopecia Areata Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient leaflet on alopecia areata causes, types, and treatment from the UK professional dermatology body.

sources.title: Gilhar A et al. Alopecia Areata. New England Journal of Medicine. 2012;366(16):1515-1525
King B et al. Two Phase 3 Trials of Baricitinib for Alopecia Areata. N Engl J Med. 2022;386(18):1687-1699
King B et al. Trial of Ritlecitinib in the Treatment of Alopecia Areata. N Engl J Med. 2023;388(11):1016-1028
Pratt CH et al. Alopecia areata. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017;3:17011
Olamiju B et al. Alopecia Areata: An Update on Diagnosis and Management. Am J Clin Dermatol. 2020;21(5):617-627
NICE Evidence Review — Baricitinib for treating severe alopecia areata (TA809)
National Alopecia Areata Foundation — Medical Treatment Options

confusedWithConditions.name: Patchy alopecia areata
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Patchy alopecia areata produces discrete oval or round bald patches anywhere on the scalp with spontaneous remission in up to 80% of cases, whereas ophiasis follows a continuous band along the scalp margins and has a far lower remission rate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Androgenic alopecia (female pattern)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Female-pattern androgenic alopecia causes diffuse widening of the central part with preserved frontal hairline; ophiasis causes complete marginal hair loss in a band without affecting the vertex.

confusedWithConditions.name: Discoid lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Discoid lupus produces scarring, scaling, plugged follicles, and permanent hair loss often affecting the scalp; ophiasis leaves smooth non-scarring skin and preserves follicle viability. Scalp biopsy readily distinguishes them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alopecia mucinosa
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alopecia mucinosa shows follicular papules and plaques with mucin deposition histologically, and can be associated with lymphoma; ophiasis has a classic dermoscopic and histological alopecia areata pattern without mucin.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia totalis
relatedConditions.relevance: Alopecia totalis is the progression of extensive alopecia areata, including ophiasis, to complete scalp hair loss; ophiasis patients have a 20-30% risk of this progression.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia universalis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most severe form, involving total body hair loss; a subset of ophiasis patients with severe, refractory disease progress to this stage.

relatedConditions.name: Alopecia areata (patchy)
relatedConditions.relevance: Ophiasis is a subtype of the alopecia areata spectrum; most ophiasis patients have a history of antecedent patchy disease.

relatedConditions.name: Pseudopelade of Brocq
relatedConditions.relevance: A scarring alopecia that can present in overlapping scalp regions; biopsy is required to distinguish non-scarring ophiasis from pseudopelade.

faqs.question: What is ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Ophiasis is a subtype of alopecia areata in which hair loss forms a continuous band along the lower scalp margins — the occipital, temporal, and parietal hairline — rather than appearing as isolated round patches. It takes its name from the Greek word for snake, reflecting the band's sinuous shape.

faqs.question: Is ophiasis the same as alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: Ophiasis is a subtype of alopecia areata that affects the scalp margins in a band pattern. Like all forms of alopecia areata, it is autoimmune and non-scarring. However, ophiasis is much harder to treat and far less likely to go into spontaneous remission than the common patchy form.

faqs.question: What causes ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Ophiasis is caused by an autoimmune attack in which T-lymphocytes target hair follicles in the scalp margin region. Genetic predisposition, atopic disease, and stress are recognised triggers. The same pathway is shared with other alopecia areata forms, but follicles at the scalp edge are particularly resistant to treatment.

faqs.question: Does ophiasis hair grow back?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous remission occurs in fewer than 10% of ophiasis cases. With JAK inhibitor treatment (baricitinib or ritlecitinib), approximately 23-38% of patients regain substantial scalp coverage. Follicles are not permanently destroyed, so regrowth remains possible even after years of hair loss.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for ophiasis?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors (baricitinib 4 mg daily in adults, ritlecitinib 50 mg daily from age 12) are the most effective treatments for severe ophiasis. Contact immunotherapy with DPCP is an alternative option with 30-60% response rates in specialist centres. Topical corticosteroids and minoxidil are adjuncts.

faqs.question: Can ophiasis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no established cure. JAK inhibitors induce meaningful regrowth in about one-third of patients while being taken, but hair typically falls out again after stopping treatment. Contact immunotherapy can produce durable remission in some patients. Management is therefore long-term.

faqs.question: Is ophiasis worse than regular alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ophiasis has a substantially worse prognosis than patchy alopecia areata. Spontaneous remission is rare (under 10%), and the band distribution responds poorly to corticosteroid injections which work better for discrete patches. Up to 30% of ophiasis patients eventually develop total scalp hair loss.

faqs.question: What does ophiasis look like?
faqs.answer: Ophiasis appears as a smooth, bald band along the lower scalp edges — at the nape, behind the ears, and across the temples. The skin in the bare area looks completely normal without scaling or scarring. Short broken 'exclamation-mark' hairs may be visible at the edge of the bald zone.

faqs.question: Is ophiasis hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a genetic component. People with a first-degree relative with alopecia areata have approximately five times the baseline risk. Specific HLA gene variants are associated with more severe disease patterns including ophiasis. However, the majority of cases occur without a clear family history.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ophiasis and tinea capitis?
faqs.answer: Tinea capitis is a fungal scalp infection causing patchy hair loss with scalp scaling, inflammation, and sometimes pustules. Ophiasis causes smooth hair loss without scaling or infection. Tinea capitis is confirmed by fungal culture and treated with antifungal drugs; ophiasis requires immunological treatment.

faqs.question: Can children get ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Childhood-onset alopecia areata is more likely to take the ophiasis or totalis pattern than adult-onset disease. Ritlecitinib has been approved for patients aged 12 and over, providing a treatment option for teenagers. Specialist paediatric dermatology referral is recommended.

faqs.question: Is ophiasis associated with other autoimmune conditions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Thyroid autoimmune disorders (particularly Hashimoto's thyroiditis), vitiligo, and type 1 diabetes co-occur with alopecia areata significantly more often than expected. Routine screening for thyroid antibodies is part of the assessment for any patient with ophiasis or other severe alopecia areata.

faqs.question: What are JAK inhibitors and how do they work for ophiasis?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors block the JAK-STAT signalling pathway that allows immune cells to attack hair follicles. By inhibiting JAK1, JAK2, or JAK3, they restore the follicle's immune privilege and allow hair to regrow. Baricitinib and ritlecitinib are the two JAK inhibitors currently approved for severe alopecia areata.

faqs.question: How long does JAK inhibitor treatment take to show results for ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Initial regrowth typically becomes visible after 12-16 weeks of treatment, with continued improvement through 36 weeks or longer. Patients are usually assessed at 6 months before deciding whether to continue. Treatment must be maintained long-term as hair loss returns in most patients who stop.

faqs.question: Are there side effects of baricitinib or ritlecitinib for ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Common side effects include upper respiratory infections and headache. More serious but less frequent concerns include elevated lipid levels, reduced blood cell counts, and, rarely, serious infections or blood clots. Regular monitoring is required. Pre-treatment screening for tuberculosis is mandatory before starting these drugs.

faqs.question: Does stress cause ophiasis?
faqs.answer: Acute psychological or physiological stress is recognised as a trigger for alopecia areata onset and relapses, though not every patient identifies a clear trigger. Stress modulates the immune environment around hair follicles. Stress management may reduce relapse frequency but is not a substitute for medical treatment.

faqs.question: Can ophiasis be confused with pattern hair loss?
faqs.answer: Female-pattern hair loss causes diffuse thinning over the crown with a preserved hairline, while ophiasis causes dense hair loss in a band specifically at the scalp margins. The locations are different. Dermoscopy readily distinguishes the two patterns and can be performed in a single clinic visit.

faqs.question: What is the sisaipho pattern?
faqs.answer: Sisaipho (ophiasis spelled backwards) is the inverse pattern — hair is lost from the central scalp while the scalp margin fringe is preserved. It is equally rare and treatment-resistant as classic ophiasis. Both patterns are considered variants of the same severe alopecia areata spectrum.

faqs.question: When should I see a dermatologist for ophiasis?
faqs.answer: See a dermatologist promptly if the bald band is widening rapidly, if you have lost eyebrows or eyelashes, or if over-the-counter treatments have not helped after 3 months. Specialist assessment can confirm the diagnosis, exclude other causes, and discuss JAK inhibitor eligibility.

faqs.question: Does ophiasis affect mental health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Depression and anxiety affect up to 40% of people with severe alopecia areata. The visible location of the alopecia band can significantly affect self-image. Psychological support, including cognitive-behavioural therapy and peer support groups, is an important part of comprehensive management.

metaDescription: Ophiasis is band-pattern alopecia areata along the scalp margins. Learn causes, JAK inhibitor treatments, and prognosis for this treatment-resistant hair loss.
